title: Is the world of Evangelion sadistic
display_title: Is the world of Evangelion sadistic
wikitext:
[]: Is the world of Evangelion sadistic?             Quote Post by Stillborn » Thu Nov 07, 2013 3:31 pm Let's make this split official and a little more tidy -NemZ It's Eva. Sadism is mandatory, wheter it's canon or Fanwork -____-

[]:             Quote Post by amitakartok » Thu Nov 07, 2013 3:56 pm Stillborn wrote: Sadism is mandatory

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Thu Nov 07, 2013 4:15 pm Adam Kadmon is called by many spiritual successor of Anno for a reason -____- If there is no sadism it's "oh noes it's not true eva! D:"

[]:             Quote Post by amitakartok » Thu Nov 07, 2013 4:27 pm That's bull and everybody knows it. If Anno is sadistic then what about the ep26 AU? Who do you think wrote that part?

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Thu Nov 07, 2013 4:31 pm It's not bull that everybody knows. Kadmon have pretty big and devoted fanbase. As for episode 26, we got EoE which showed what really happened outside of Shinji's momentary happy hallucination. More on Reticence topic... Is it just me or does Asuka asking at one point of her rant, what kind of God/creator would put them through this, sounds almost like knocking on fourth wall and accusation towards author(s)?

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Nov 07, 2013 5:46 pm Stillborn wrote: Adam Kadmon is called by many spiritual successor of Anno for a reason

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Thu Nov 07, 2013 10:46 pm I got the same implication, Stillborn. That jumped out at me as being a deliberately 'fourth wall' observation, and I liked it. I also have to disagree with the 'sadism' part. I think that Eva is certainly a much easier IP to fall into in terms of tragedy, but I don't know if Anno wanted tragedy for his characters, at least in the sense that he felt like he was punishing himself or others through his characters, and using them as proxies. Whenever I read something about Anno, he seems like the kind of guy that likes...lighthearted stuff. And when you get to how many people can take extremely light hearted IP's (hell, Disney IP's get this treatment!) and turn them into dark, depressing (but sometimes very good) versions of themselves, I don't see why Evangelion can't have optimistic fanfics. In fact, I think you can still have a dark, tragic, heartwrenching fic, and still put an optimistic ending to it. There's catharsis when that's done correctly.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Thu Nov 07, 2013 11:08 pm Gob Hobblin wrote: And when you get to how many people can take extremely light hearted IP's (hell, Disney IP's get this treatment!) and turn them into dark, depressing (but sometimes very good) versions of themselves, I don't see why Evangelion can't have optimistic fanfics. In fact, I think you can still have a dark, tragic, heartwrenching fic, and still put an optimistic ending to it. There's catharsis when that's done correctly.

[]:             Quote Post by NemZ » Fri Nov 08, 2013 1:52 am Bagheera wrote: This is most obvious in EoE: even after the brutality of that movie nobody dies.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Fri Nov 08, 2013 1:56 am That's largely dependent on how you view Instrumentality: is it death, or transfiguration? The terms used to describe the event are ambiguous enough that it's open to personal interpretation. So, technically, everyone could be dead, and the world is over...or everyone could be alive (even those that died in the course of the movie, being 'saved' by Instrumentality), and their eventual return all but assured. That's the strength of the ending: it's completely up to the viewer to decide.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Fri Nov 08, 2013 2:11 am Bagheera wrote: even after the brutality of that movie nobody dies.

[]:             Quote Post by Atropos » Fri Nov 08, 2013 6:07 am I respect your intepretations, but I believe this (was) the wrong thread for them.

[]:             Quote Post by Bagheera » Fri Nov 08, 2013 7:12 am Mr. Tines wrote: In a situation, as was said of the aftermath of global nuclear war, where the living would envy the dead, that's sadism.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Fri Nov 08, 2013 10:36 am There's also a misinterpretation of the word sadism in here that needs to be addressed: sadism implies intent on the part of the harmful party, as well as enjoyment in causing the harm. Those two have to be working in combination for there to be any element of sadism. There are certainly sadistic elements to the NGE story (whether some of them are deliberate or not is up to debate, but some of those elements certainly are). At it's core, however, NGE is a cosmic horror story: the overall elements that can bring about destruction ultimately don't care very much about humans, see them (or individuals) as a hindrance to natural order and the way things ought to be. They can cause what seem like sadistic acts to us (mindraping Asuka, Tabris playing with Shinji), but the entities themselves don't seem to derive much pleasure from this. They are simply acting in concert with their nature.

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Fri Nov 08, 2013 10:49 am I never meant that angels are sadistic. They are tools that conveys the sadism of the creator. Be it canon or fanwork. What I meant is that the author must use those tools, to break, cripple torture and destroy the lives of characters, for the enjoyment of "fans" otherwise it's not "true Evangelion". Just look how many felt offended with poka Rei. That she is moe and what not. In other words: She was starting to open up too quickly for them. She was starting to enjoy life. "But It's heresy!! D:!" That's what I meant that Eva must be sadistic by default. Otherwise "it's not deep enough D:" -____-

[]:             Quote Post by Gemini011 » Fri Nov 08, 2013 1:04 pm Stillborn wrote: What I meant is that the author must use those tools, to break, cripple torture and destroy the lives of characters, for the enjoyment of "fans" otherwise it's not "true Evangelion".

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Fri Nov 08, 2013 1:10 pm I don't think that it's sadistic, because I don't think that Anno or anybody working on the project is getting off on the idea of killing off or otherwise causing brutal harm to any of their characters.

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Fri Nov 08, 2013 1:22 pm Hostel series? Saw? They are not sadistic since no one from the production team is fapping to them right? They are just there to make cash by depicting gruesome torture made in the name of actually nothing. Bible is one of the most sadistic books I've seen. Mainly Old Testament. God is giving cruel trials to humans to test them... Despite already knowing the answer from the start, making whole excercise pointless aside from admission of blind faith. In the end it's just a form of tortureporn in various form. And Anno is acting like a god here. He is torturing his creations for the sake of some test and message, that either contradicts itself or is hollow when we try to apply it to our lives. But his characters will still accept whatever comes to them as some sort of divine wisdom at the end of the movie, despite being irreversibly screwed for little to none reason. And that course of action applies to everyone who wants to write "true Eva". The difference between fanwork and canon is that Anno is getting ritch on that, since someone being eaten alive, minutes after finding a reason to live is "oh so deep". Fanwork just like to make people suffer for the sake of suffering.

[]:             Quote Post by Nuclear Lunchbox » Fri Nov 08, 2013 1:38 pm Yes, Saw is not sadistic. I can't say anything about the people watching them, though. Doing something for the sake of story is not sadistic. Likewise, the Bible itself is not a sadistic work. The Dictionary wrote: Sadistic adj. : Deriving pleasure from inflicting pain, suffering, or humiliation on others.

[]:             Quote Post by Gob Hobblin » Fri Nov 08, 2013 1:43 pm But Stillborn, that's ignoring the root definition of sadism: the derivation of pleasure from causing pain. There isn't much in the Bible to indicate God was pleased with what He was doing (sorry, have to capitalize, it's...I'm a Presby). Those trials could be considered to be strengthening exercises, to give those being tested (or more appropriately, those reading about the trials) a profound truth to be earned at the end of it. That's the same as any other myth involving human suffering for a goal (frankly, if you want to talk religious sadism, I would point to Hellenic mythology, where the gods are positively gleeful in their agendas against us mere mortals). Now, if you're suggesting we ourselves are sadists for enjoying what we see, that falls flat, as well, because we are not causing the pain: we're observing it. That's schadenfreude, and a whole separate beast (the derivation of pleasure from observed pain or misfortune, as opposed to caused pain or misfortune). You make a good point on the artist being a sadist, and there may be a case for that. I can think of Drag Me To Hell as the more deliberate invocation of this, since the protagonist was pretty harmless and largely innocent and undeserving of her misfortune...but it was clear that Sam Raimi absolutely adored finding new torments to put her through. He admitted this, and he is, pretty much, a sadist (though largely a harmless, hucksterish sadist...I could go on about the things he did to put Bruce Campbell in harm's way, and they are admittedly hysterical. Even Bruce thinks they are). Does Anno enjoy tormenting his characters? There could be a case for that. Sometimes, some of his comments seem to imply as such, whether the characters are surrogates for him, people he knows, the fans...he likes tormenting them. Truthfully, I could argue that the real target of his torment is us, the fans: like Hideo Kojima, he seems to take glee in building our expectations and then dashing them against the rocks, knowing that (accurately), we will still flock to him lovingly time and again. As for the characters themselves...it's hard to say. In the original series, he really didn't seem to enjoy Evangelion towards the end, and it's doubtful he enjoyed torturing his characters. I think the torments were more of a catharsis for him, as opposed to an outright pleasure: he felt he himself needed to be punished for being an awful person (at least, I'm assuming this, since this is how the depressives in my own life rationalize their self-loathing, and Shinji was his stand-in. He didn't enjoy tormenting Shinji: he did it, because he thought it was necessary. It was required. That's not really sadistic, more...compulsory. Edit: And Nuke-kun beat me to the punch, summing it up more succinctly. Cure you, Nuke-kun. Curse you and your tiny, to-the-point posts. Why are they so tiny!?

[]:             Quote Post by amitakartok » Thu Nov 07, 2013 3:56 pm Stillborn wrote: Sadism is mandatory
